html:
Tear-Off and Replacement for Roofs That Have Run Out of Options

Some roofs are past the point where a coating or recover buys meaningful time. Multiple layers, a saturated deck, or a system that no manufacturer will warranty anymore all point to a full tear-off. We scope that decision from actual core samples and moisture data, not a visual walk alone.

Reading the Roof Before Committing to a Method

A tear-off starts with core cuts at several points across the roof field, not only near obvious problem areas. On a building with a long service history, the assembly can vary from one section to the next if past repairs added a patch system over only part of the roof.

Infrared or capacitance moisture scanning maps out where insulation has already failed, which lets us mark tear-off boundaries before a crew ever opens the roof. That map goes into the proposal so a facilities team can see exactly what's driving the scope and cost.

We also review the roof's permit and repair history where records exist, since a building that's already carried two prior recover layers is often at the code ceiling regardless of what the moisture scan shows. That history changes the conversation from whether a tear-off makes sense to how it gets phased.

Aged Built-Up Roofs on the County's Mill-Era Buildings

Downtown Spartanburg and the mill villages around Startex, Arcadia, and Clifton still carry a fair number of built-up roofs from the textile era, some with gravel ballast over multiple bitumen plies. These assemblies were built for a slower repair cycle than modern low-slope systems, and by the time they need full replacement the wood deck underneath has usually absorbed decades of slow moisture intrusion.

On these buildings we plan for deck repair as a standard line item rather than a contingency, since finding sound plywood or plank everywhere on a mill-era roof is the exception, not the rule. Where steel joists have any surface corrosion from long-term leaks, that gets flagged for a structural engineer before we close the deck back up.

Buildings around Startex, Arcadia, and Clifton also tend to have irregular roof geometry from additions built onto the original mill footprint over the decades, which means a single tear-off often crosses several different original construction eras on one job. We map those transitions before demolition starts so the crew isn't discovering a new deck type mid-day.

Sequencing Tear-Off Around an Operating Building

Every tear-off is broken into sections sized to what can be stripped, decked, insulated, and dried in with a watertight membrane before the crew leaves for the day. No section of roof is left open overnight regardless of the forecast.

On active manufacturing floors, tear-off sections are staged to avoid dust and debris falling directly over sensitive equipment or open production lines. We coordinate the day's zone with the plant's operations lead each morning so the work area lines up with what's running below.

What a Tear-Off Scope Documents

  • Number of existing roof layers and their composition
  • Deck material, span, and any areas requiring structural repair
  • Moisture scan results and tear-off boundary map
  • Disposal and hauling plan for removed material
  • Daily dry-in sequence and weather contingency plan
  • New system specification and manufacturer warranty term

Deck Repair and Structural Coordination

Where deck sections come out rotted or delaminated, replacement lumber or steel deck panels are matched to the existing framing spacing so load paths stay consistent with the original design. We photograph deck condition before and after repair for the closeout file.

On buildings with any history of roof-related structural concerns, we bring in a structural engineer to sign off on repairs before insulation goes back down. That step adds time to the schedule but protects the facility's asset records and any future sale or refinance inspection.

Debris Handling and Site Logistics

Tear-off generates a substantial volume of debris, and on active industrial sites that material has to move without blocking dock doors, fire lanes, or employee parking. We stage roll-off containers and hoist locations with the facility ahead of mobilization so the plan is set before crews arrive.

Where a building sits close to neighboring tenants, as many do in the industrial parks along the I-85 corridor, we coordinate noise-generating work like deck demolition to hours that won't conflict with a neighbor's own operating schedule.

Tear-Off Questions We Hear Most Often

How do you know if a roof needs tear-off instead of a recover?

Core samples and moisture scans tell the story. If the insulation is wet across a meaningful share of the roof, or the assembly already has the maximum layers code allows, recover isn't an option regardless of how the surface looks.

Will the deck need to be replaced entirely?

Rarely does an entire deck need replacement. Most tear-offs find isolated sections of damage that get repaired in place while the sound deck stays untouched.

How much roof can a crew tear off and dry in per day?

It depends on crew size, roof access, and weather, but the section size is always set conservatively enough that dry-in finishes before the workday ends.

What happens if rain moves in during a tear-off?

Work is sequenced so any open section is already dried in before a forecast window closes. If conditions shift unexpectedly, temporary protection goes down immediately rather than waiting out the shift.

Does tear-off require notifying tenants or neighboring businesses?

For multi-tenant buildings or sites near other operations, we ask the property manager how they want tenants notified and build the schedule around any noise or access restrictions they flag.
